title: Life before the scan
summary: Before cancer was part of my vocabulary, life was already full: travel, love, work, plans, and the quiet assumption that tomorrow would mostly resemble today.
body: The diagnosis did not create my life. It interrupted one that was already moving.

media.caption: Life before the diagnosis.

title: A life already in motion
summary: There was a whole life before the scan reports arrived: love, travel, work, and plans that had nothing to do with cancer.
body: I want the story to keep returning to this truth. The diagnosis interrupted my life; it did not become the whole of it.

media.caption: Life before cancer entered the story.

title: Before the medical language arrived
summary: Ordinary days, travel, love, and plans came before scan reports and treatment names became part of the calendar.
body: This is the part I do not want swallowed by the diagnosis: a life already moving, already full, already mine.

media.caption: A life already in motion.

title: One word in an app: lesion
summary: I found out by email while I was at dinner with my wife. I opened the app, saw the word lesion, and had to Google what it meant.
body: The strange part was that I had no symptoms. A whole-body MRI had unexpectedly found a non-enhancing right frontal lesion, and one unfamiliar word turned into one of the biggest decisions of my life: how to find the right doctor when everything was suddenly on the line.

media.caption: Axial FLAIR-style brain MRI clip.

title: The search moved into ordinary life
summary: The strange part was looking and feeling mostly normal while one word from a scan report had changed everything underneath.
body: The first days were not cinematic. They were dinners, messages, tabs, calls, documents, and a private search process starting to take over the background of normal life.

media.caption: Personal image from the early diagnosis window.

title: I built a doctor search engine
summary: I treated finding a brain surgeon like a system: broad search, doctor-to-doctor reputation, then real consults.
body: I made a sheet for the logistics everyone kept asking for, then another for the real question: who had actually spent years on this exact condition? I started with 23 doctors and looked for publications, clinical trials, specialization, and reputation inside medicine, then narrowed the list through conversations and consults.
facts: Decision rule: choose honesty over comfort

media.caption: Illustration of the doctor-search system: records, reputation, cities, and consults.

title: Right frontal craniotomy and gross total resection
summary: Resection described as gross total/near-total for an astrocytoma, IDH-mutant, CNS WHO grade 2.
body: My records place the surgery and pathology around September 1 to September 5, 2023: right frontal craniotomy, resection described as gross total/near-total, and pathology consistent with astrocytoma, IDH-mutant, CNS WHO grade 2.

media.caption: Short artifact from the surgery date.

title: The postoperative chapter begins
summary: Recovery began with a new physical reality: healing from surgery while the postoperative cavity became the new baseline on scans.
body: The notes describe a fluid-filled postoperative cavity and minimal surrounding T2/FLAIR change. Outside the report language, this was the beginning of learning what recovery felt like day to day.

media.caption: Recovery after surgery.

title: The visible part of recovery
summary: Recovery was not only something in the radiology report. It was visible in the mirror, in the scar, and in the slow return to ordinary days.
body: The scans had their own language for the postoperative cavity. My body had another one: stitches, hair, fatigue, and the strange feeling of carrying proof of surgery around with me.

media.caption: The visible early recovery chapter.

title: The tumor gets a molecular identity
summary: The cancer stopped being only a scan and a grade for me. It became IDH1 R132C, ATRX, TP53, MGMT, TMB, MSI, and a set of clues I could actually learn from.
body: The molecular picture changed how I understood my cancer. It was still personal and terrifying, but now there were specific mutations, biomarkers, and uncertainties I could take into research, second opinions, and treatment conversations.

media.caption: Illustration of the tumor's core molecular identity.

title: The three-month MRI rhythm begins
summary: Residual fluid-filled postoperative bed; minimal wispy peri-resection enhancement unchanged.
body: Surveillance meant returning to the scanner every few months: schedule the MRI, wait, read the report, exhale if it says stable, then start the cycle again. No masslike enhancement was reported here.

media.caption: Illustration of stable MRI surveillance and watchful waiting.

title: Portugal, trying to enjoy life
summary: After surgery and early surveillance, we went to Portugal and tried to let life be bigger than the diagnosis.
body: There was still fear in the background, but I did not want every month to become a medical month. Portugal was me trying to enjoy the life I still had in front of me, even while the next decisions were starting to form.

media.caption: Portugal, trying to enjoy life between appointments.

title: The Camino trips
summary: Walking became its own kind of answer: not a cure, not a medical milestone, but a way to keep moving through uncertainty.
body: After surgery and early surveillance, I wanted proof that my body was still capable of forward motion. The Camino was not treatment; it was life refusing to become only treatment.
facts: A personal milestone, not a medical endpoint

media.caption: A travel moment from the Camino chapter.

title: Still choosing the road
summary: The Camino became a way to make forward motion literal before the next treatment chapter accelerated.
body: There were scans, records, medication decisions, and vaccine planning in the background. There was also walking, weather, distance, and the stubborn fact that life was still happening.

media.caption: On the Camino route.

title: Another day forward
summary: Another day of walking, before IDH inhibitor therapy and the vaccine schedule became the center of the calendar.
body: This part matters because it is not a medical endpoint. It is a reminder that the timeline includes effort, movement, and ordinary beauty alongside the medical decisions.

media.caption: Another Camino route image.

title: Ivosidenib begins
summary: The records place the ivosidenib start in late June 2024 as the first IDH inhibitor chapter.
body: This is where treatment started to become a daily reality: targeted therapy, monitoring, adverse effects, and changing course when my body objected.

media.caption: Illustration of targeted IDH therapy and careful monitoring.

title: Vaccine priming starts in Tübingen
summary: My first personalized peptide vaccine doses happened July 9-12, 2024.
body: The first four doses happened across July 9, 10, 11, and 12 with Leukine and Imiquimod, with the July 12 visit also used for an immune-monitoring blood draw.

media.caption: Illustration of the personal neoantigen vaccine priming phase.

title: Ivosidenib discontinued
summary: Ivosidenib stopped because of chest pain / angina documentation.
body: The first IDH-inhibitor chapter ended because my body objected. The record says chest pain and angina; the lived version is a treatment decision that had to be changed quickly.

media.caption: Illustration of treatment adjustment after side effects.

title: We started building Triangle
summary: Triangle started in September 2024, after we raised the money to turn the patient-navigation process I had built for myself into a real product.
body: The idea came from living the problem: fragmented records, medical jargon, treatment options buried across papers and trials, and the unfair advantage of knowing how to work the system. September 2024 was when that personal operating system became the beginning of the Triangle build.
facts: A product version of the system I needed

media.caption: Early Triangle build moment.

title: Vorasidenib starts
summary: Vorasidenib 40 mg daily began as the alternative IDH inhibitor.
body: Vorasidenib became one of the anchors of what I was doing, with liver-function monitoring and temporary hold/resume moments later on. The idea was two-pronged: inhibit mutant IDH activity while the exploratory vaccine tried to help T cells recognize selected tumor-specific peptide targets.

media.caption: Illustration of ongoing IDH inhibition and monitoring.

title: First clear vaccine response
summary: Four-month monitoring detected CD4+ responses to 5 of 17 peptides and CD8+ responses to 4 of 17 peptides.
body: The report summarized this as specific T-cell response to 8 of the 17 vaccinated mutation-spanning peptides. I read this as an important early immune-readout moment: not proof of clinical benefit, but evidence that my immune system had measurable responses to parts of the vaccine cocktail.

media.caption: Illustration of measured T-cell response to vaccine peptides.

title: No masslike enhancing recurrence
summary: MRI brain reported no residual or recurrent masslike enhancement.
body: Another surveillance scan, another wait for language that could change everything. This one stayed calm: no residual or recurrent masslike enhancement.

media.caption: Illustration of surveillance imaging showing stability.

title: Ninth vaccine dose while clinically well
summary: CeCurio documented the 9th personalized peptide vaccination on March 10, 2025.
body: The visit note recorded ECOG 0 and KPS 100%, no reported disease symptoms, current vorasidenib treatment, and the last MRI without signs of tumor in December 2024. The vaccine was administered with Leukine and Imiquimod.

media.caption: Illustration of ongoing personal neoantigen vaccine dosing.

title: Vorasidenib held for elevated LFTs
summary: ALT/AST elevations led to a temporary vorasidenib hold.
body: Treatment was not just the pill. It was monitoring, thresholds, holds, and restarts: the invisible maintenance work around trying to stay on therapy safely.

media.caption: Illustration of medication monitoring and lab-guided treatment hold.

title: Ten-month vaccine response persists
summary: Ten months after the first vaccine, immune monitoring detected responses to 9 of 18 vaccinated mutation-spanning peptides.
body: The June 23, 2025 immune-monitoring report described CD4+ T-cell responses to 5 of 18 tested peptides and CD8+ T-cell responses to 6 of 18 tested peptides. For me, this makes the vaccine story more concrete than a design idea: there are longitudinal immune readouts.

media.caption: Illustration of persistent vaccine-linked immune responses.

title: Vorasidenib resumed
summary: Vorasidenib resumed after improvement in liver function tests.
body: The treatment story is not linear; it is adjusted, watched, resumed, and re-checked.

media.caption: Illustration of restarting vorasidenib after lab improvement.

title: One-year vaccine response still detectable
summary: One year and one month after the first vaccine, specific T-cell responses to at least 5 of 18 vaccinated mutation-spanning peptides were still detectable.
body: The report also noted low measured CD4+/CD8+ cell numbers and/or viability after in-vitro culture and recommended repeating the assay. I want that caveat next to the result: still detectable, but with sample-quality limits.

media.caption: Illustration of one-year immune monitoring after the personal neoantigen vaccine.

title: Triangle becomes a real company
summary: By December 2025, the patient-navigation system I had built for myself had become real work.
body: It was no longer just a private spreadsheet and operating system. Triangle had become a product effort around complete records, AI-powered research, and a doctor in the loop.

media.caption: Illustration of Triangle moving from personal system to public product.

title: The doctor-search system became the deeper lesson
summary: The surgeon search made one thing obvious: the healthcare system works best for people who already know how to work the system.
body: I started with a simple sheet, then turned it into a search process: a broad list, publications and trials, reputation inside medicine, and consults that showed me how each doctor thought. The final decision rule was not who made me feel safest in the moment. It was who corrected me, pushed back, and helped me make the right decision.

media.caption: Illustration of the doctor-search process that later shaped Triangle.

title: Stable on surveillance
summary: Ongoing follow-up documented stability on MRI while I stayed on vorasidenib and immunotherapy.
body: By this point, stability had become its own kind of work: scans, labs, treatment, and trying to keep living in the space between appointments.
facts: No masslike or nodular enhancing recurrence reported
ALT/AST improved relative to prior peaks

media.caption: Illustration of continued stable surveillance.

title: Still moving forward
summary: Near the end of the timeline, I wanted one more image that is not a scan, a lab, or a company screenshot.
body: It belongs here because the point was never only to survive the medical sequence. It was to keep a life large enough that the cancer story could sit inside it without becoming all of it.

media.caption: Still moving forward.

title: The thing I needed is now a real product
summary: The ending for now is simple: Triangle is on screen.
body: The system I wished I had during diagnosis has become a product other patients can use.

media.caption: Triangle home with health topics and patient context.
Triangle genome view showing body-system genetic context.
